source.title: Adaptive laboratory evolution accelerated glutarate production by Corynebacterium glutamicum

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aimed to improve glutarate production by Corynebacterium glutamicum using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) with flux enforcement. The initial problem was low volumetric productivity of a metabolically engineered strain (GluA) producing glutarate via a synthetic pathway. The hypothesis was that coupling growth to glutarate production by deleting gdh (encoding glutamate dehydrogenase) would allow selection of faster-growing mutants with improved production. A serendipitous discovery of a faster-growing variant (GluA T0) with a GabD mutation prompted a systematic ALE experiment. Over serial transfers, growth rate and productivity increased, plateauing at transfer 7. Genome sequencing and reverse genetics identified causal mutations, notably gltB^E686Q, whose benefit depended on gdh deletion. The evolved strain GluA T7 showed improved performance in bioreactors, and downstream processing was optimized.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: ALE was used to improve a C. glutamicum strain engineered for glutarate production by flux enforcement via gdh deletion. Faster-growing mutants were isolated, with growth rates increasing from 0.10 to 0.17 h⁻¹. The fastest mutant showed twofold higher volumetric productivity (0.18 g L⁻¹ h⁻¹). Genome sequencing revealed candidate mutations; reverse genetics identified an amino acid exchange in GltB as causal, dependent on gdh deletion. In a 2 L bioreactor, the evolved mutant achieved 22.7 g L⁻¹ titer, 0.23 g g⁻¹ yield, and 0.35 g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ productivity. Reactive extraction was optimized.
process_signals: Hypothesis: strains selected for faster glutarate-coupled growth show improved production.
ALE experiment isolated faster-growing mutants.
Reverse genetics confirmed causal mutation.
Bioreactor performance validated scalability.
methods: Adaptive laboratory evolution with flux enforcement (gdh deletion)

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Glutarate is a C5-dicarboxylic acid used for polyamides. It can be produced from L-lysine via four pathways, all converging to 5AVA then to glutarate via GabT and GabD. The pathway using LdcC-PatA-PatD-GabT-GabD couples glutarate production to transaminase reactions generating L-glutamate. Deleting gdh enforces flux by making growth dependent on glutarate production, as transaminases become crucial for nitrogen assimilation. ALE can leverage natural selection to optimize production without prior genetic knowledge. The study aimed to accelerate glutarate production by ALE with flux enforcement.
process_signals: Flux enforcement concept: gdh deletion couples growth to glutarate production.
ALE is suitable when a growth advantage can be selected.
Prior work achieved high titers but needed productivity improvement.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution of flux enforced glutarate production improved volumetric productivity by metabolically engineered C. glutamicum
section_gists.gist: Serendipitously, a variant (GluA T0) with a GabD^P134L mutation showed faster growth (μ=0.12 vs 0.05 h⁻¹) and higher glutarate (45 vs 25 mM). GabD^P134L had higher activity at 10-20 mM glutarate but was still feedback-inhibited at higher concentrations. This prompted an ALE experiment starting from GluA T0. Serial transfers (T0 to T8) were performed, with growth rate increasing from 0.10 to 0.17 h⁻¹ by T5, plateauing thereafter. Glutarate titer increased 10% and 5AVA tenfold by T7. Volumetric productivity doubled from 0.09 to 0.18 g L⁻¹ h⁻¹.
process_signals: Serendipitous discovery of GabD^P134L mutation improving growth and production.
ALE design: serial transfer, selecting for faster growth under flux enforcement.
Growth rate plateaued at T5, but product titers continued to improve until T7.
Volumetric productivity doubled.
methods: Enzyme activity assays with feedback inhibition testing
Phyre2 modeling and CUPSAT stability prediction
failures_or_limits: GabD^P134L still subject to feedback inhibition by glutarate at high concentrations.
Growth rate improvement plateaued after T5.

section_gists.heading: RNAseq analysis of global gene expression patterns in ALE strains GluA T5 and GluA T7
section_gists.gist: RNAseq compared GluA T5, GluA T7, and GluA T0. In GluA T5, 52 genes downregulated (e.g., sucCD, aceA) and 62 upregulated (e.g., transporters). In GluA T7, 107 genes downregulated, many in AmtR regulon (nitrogen starvation response), and yggB (glutamate exporter) downregulated in both. No changes in synthetic pathway genes. Transcriptome not immediately instructive for explaining improved production.
process_signals: Transcriptome analysis did not directly explain improved production.
Downregulation of nitrogen starvation genes in GluA T7 suggests altered nitrogen assimilation.
yggB downregulation may reduce glutamate loss, enhancing flux enforcement.
methods: RNA sequencing and differential expression analysis
failures_or_limits: Transcriptome analysis was not instructive to explain improved glutarate production at first sight.

section_gists.heading: Genome sequencing and reverse genetics for identification of mutations causal for improved glutarate production by ALE strains GluA T5 and GluA T7
section_gists.gist: Genome sequencing of GluA T5 revealed no mutations but increased plasmid copy numbers (PCN) for all three plasmids. GluA T7 had comparable PCN for two plasmids, higher for the third, and two mutations: a 21-bp deletion in tetA(Z) on the plasmid, and a SNP gltB^E686Q in the chromosome. Reverse genetics: introducing the mutated plasmid alone (GluA RG1) reduced performance; introducing gltB^E686Q alone (GluA RG2) improved growth and productivity to levels comparable to GluA T7. Combining both (GluA RG3) did not further improve. Thus, gltB^E686Q is the causal mutation.
process_signals: Surprising finding: GluA T5 had no mutations, only increased PCN.
GluA T7 had two mutations; reverse genetics showed gltB^E686Q is causal.
Mutated plasmid alone was detrimental.
Combining mutations did not add benefit beyond gltB^E686Q.
methods: Reverse genetic engineering (introducing mutations individually and combined)
failures_or_limits: GluA T5 had no genomic mutations, only PCN increase, which was unexpected.
Mutated plasmid (tetA(Z) deletion) reduced antibiotic resistance and did not improve production alone.

section_gists.heading: The beneficial effect of GltB^E686Q depends on the deletion of gdh
section_gists.gist: Tested if GltB^E686Q benefit requires gdh deletion or the specific glutarate pathway. Introduced mutation into L-lysine overproducer GSLA2 (with and without gdh deletion). Without gdh deletion, GltB^E686Q did not change lysine production. With gdh deletion, GltB^E686Q restored lysine production to levels of the gdh+ strain. Thus, the benefit is dependent on gdh deletion and independent of the glutarate pathway.
process_signals: Hypothesis: GltB^E686Q benefit is conditional on gdh deletion.
Confirmed: mutation only beneficial in gdh deletion background.
Benefit extends to L-lysine production, not just glutarate.
methods: Strain construction (introducing gltB^E686Q into different backgrounds)

section_gists.heading: Fermentative glutarate production by evolved strain GluA T7 in comparison to GluA T0
section_gists.gist: In batch bioreactors, GluA T0 grew at 0.11 h⁻¹, reached 13.1 g/L CDW, 2.0 g/L glutarate, productivity 0.05 g/L/h. GluA T7 grew at 0.19 h⁻¹, reached 10.2 g/L CDW, 10.2 g/L glutarate, productivity 0.21 g/L/h. In fed-batch, GluA T7 achieved 22.7 g/L glutarate, yield 0.23 g/g, productivity 0.35 g/L/h. Performance was stable.
process_signals: Scalability validated: evolved strain performed better in bioreactor.
Fed-batch further improved titer and productivity.
Stable performance over cultivation.
methods: Comparison of evolved vs parental strain

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Flux enforcement enabled ALE to improve productivity. Causal mutation gltB^E686Q in GOGAT was identified; its benefit depends on gdh deletion. The mutation may allow GOGAT to function at higher ammonia concentrations, reducing nitrogen starvation response. Downregulation of yggB may prevent glutamate loss. The approach complements previous strategies and can be combined with other optimizations. Limitations: mechanism of GltB^E686Q not fully understood; further engineering possible.
process_signals: First use of ALE with flux enforcement for glutarate.
GltB^E686Q is a novel target not previously engineered.
Hypothesis: mutation alters GOGAT activity under high ammonia.
yggB downregulation may enhance flux enforcement.
Approach can be combined with other metabolic engineering strategies.
failures_or_limits: Mechanism of GltB^E686Q not fully elucidated.
Further improvements possible by combining with other strategies.

section_gists.heading: Material and methods
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Molecular biology methods
section_gists.gist: Standard methods for DNA isolation, cloning, transformation. Suicide vector pK19mobsacB used for gene deletion and point mutation introduction via Gibson assembly. Primers listed in Table 4.
process_signals: Used Gibson assembly for introducing SNP gltB^E686Q.
Suicide vector for chromosomal modifications.

section_gists.heading: Availability of data and materials
section_gists.gist: Data available in article and additional files. Genome sequencing data: BioProject PRJNA691520. Transcriptomic data: ArrayExpress E-MTAB-10025.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Flux enforcement by gdh deletion couples growth to glutarate production, enabling selection for improved production via ALE.
Serendipitous GabD^P134L mutation provided an initial improvement, motivating systematic ALE.
ALE would yield mutations that increase growth rate and productivity under selection pressure.
Genome sequencing would reveal causal mutations, which could be validated by reverse genetics.
The beneficial effect of gltB^E686Q is specific to the gdh deletion background, as it likely improves nitrogen assimilation via GS/GOGAT.
Evolved strains would perform better in scalable bioreactor conditions.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Exact molecular mechanism by which GltB^E686Q improves GOGAT activity and glutarate production.
Why GluA T5 showed improved phenotype without genomic mutations (only PCN increase).
Whether further ALE rounds could overcome the growth rate plateau.
Potential of combining gltB^E686Q with other known engineering targets (e.g., ynfM overexpression) for synergistic effects.
Long-term genetic stability of the evolved strain in industrial conditions.

events.summary: The study aimed to accelerate glutarate production by evolutionary engineering.
The hypothesis that strains selected for faster glutarate-coupled growth by ALE show improved glutarate production was tested.
Genome sequencing of the evolved strain revealed candidate mutations for improved production.
Reverse genetic engineering revealed that an amino acid exchange in the large subunit of L-glutamic acid-2-oxoglutarate aminotransferase was causal for accelerated glutarate production.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains the abstract and introduction, which outline the problem, hypothesis, experimental design, key results, and validation. Events are extracted from explicit statements in the text.

events.summary: ALE transfer experiment: serial transfers from GluA T0 to T8, selecting for faster growth under flux enforcement.
GluA T7 accumulated 10% more glutarate and tenfold more 5AVA than parent.
Transcriptome analysis was not instructive to explain improved glutarate production.
Genome sequencing of GluA T5 revealed no mutations but increased plasmid copy numbers.
Genome sequencing of GluA T7 identified two mutations: a 21-bp deletion in tetA(Z) and SNP gltB^E686Q.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers ALE transfer experiment, growth/productivity characterization, RNAseq analysis, and genome sequencing results for GluA T5 and T7.

events.summary: Tested GltB^E686Q in L-lysine overproducer with and without gdh deletion; mutation only beneficial in gdh deletion background.
numbers_or_metrics: L-lysine decreased by 38% upon gdh deletion
GSLA2G::gltB^E686Q produced 54 mM L-lysine vs 29 mM for GSLA2G

events.summary: Batch bioreactor comparison: GluA T7 outperformed GluA T0 in growth rate, titer, productivity, and yield.

events.summary: Reactive extraction optimization: T-C6 with 10 wt% 1-dodecanol in ethyl oleate achieved 58.1% glutarate yield at molar ratio 9/1.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers reverse genetics validation, dependency of gltB^E686Q on gdh deletion, bioreactor performance comparison, and downstream processing optimization.

events.summary: Re-extraction of glutarate from organic phase using water-soluble amines n-propylamine (M-C3) and n-butylamine (M-C4) achieved up to 99% yield.
Discussion notes that flux enforcement coupling growth to L-glutamic acid production enabled selection of faster-growing strains via ALE, doubling volumetric productivity.
Full recovery of glutarate from fermentation broth not achieved in initial reactive extraction due to strong electrolytes like chloride ions competing with amine extractant.
Sulfuric acid chosen to adjust pH before reactive extraction to mitigate competition from chloride ions and improve extraction yield.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers downstream processing (reactive extraction/re-extraction) and discussion of ALE results, including hypotheses on GltB^E686Q mechanism and limitations in extraction.

events.summary: ALE experiment design: repeated batch cultivations transferring the replicate with highest cell density in exponential phase to fresh medium.
Whole-genome sequencing performed on originating strain GluA T0 and evolved strains GluA T5 and GluA T7 to identify mutations.
Coupled in vitro activity assay of GabT and GabD to compare GabD variants under glutarate feedback inhibition.
In vitro activity assay of GOGAT (GltBD) at different glutamine concentrations to determine Km.
Fermentative production in batch and fed-batch bioreactors to evaluate evolved strain performance.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for ALE, sequencing, enzyme assays, bioreactor fermentations, and tetracycline MIC, which are core experimental procedures supporting the paper's evolution events.

events.summary: Genome sequencing and SNP detection were performed using Illumina and Nanopore technologies, with hybrid assembly via Unicycler.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk primarily describes detailed methods for genome sequencing, RNAseq, reactive extraction, and supplementary experiments. It contains experimental events but no major problem/hypothesis/design events.
This chunk contains only the reference list and publisher's note, with no research process events.

evidence_bank.quote: In this study, we aimed to accelerate glutarate production by evolutionary engineering.
The hypothesis that strains selected for faster glutarate-coupled growth by adaptive laboratory evolution show improved glutarate production was tested.
Genome sequencing of the evolved strain revealed candidate mutations for improved production.

Reverse genetic engineering revealed that an amino acid exchange in the large subunit of L-glutamic acid-2-oxoglutarate aminotransferase was causal for accelerated glutarate production and its beneficial effect was dependent on flux enforcement due to deletion of gdh.

At every transfer, one aliquot was streaked out on agar plates and three single colonies were selected and used to make glycerol stocks (triplicate glycerol stocks GluA T1 until triplicate glycerol stocks GluA T8).
ALE strain GluA T7 accumulated 10% more glutarate and tenfold more 5AVA (20±1 mM compared to 2±0 mM) than the parent strain
At first sight, the transcriptome analysis was not instructive to explain the improved glutarate production of the strains selected by ALE.
Surprisingly, genome sequencing of strain GluA T5 did neither reveal genome-based nor plasmid-based mutations in comparison to the non-evolved strain GluA T0.
analysis of the sequence reads mapped to DNA of the three expression plasmids showed increased plasmid copy numbers (PCN) of all three plasmids.
two mutations were identified in GluA T7 that were absent from strains GluA T0 and GluA T5.
a deletion of 21 bp in the coding sequence of the antibiotic resistance marker of this plasmid, i.e. in for tetracycline efflux permease gene tetA(Z)
As a consequence of the gdh deletion, production of L-lysine was decreased by about 38%
GluA T7 clearly outcompeted its precursor strain regarding product titer, volumetric productivity, and yield

The results clearly showed that with increasing T-C6 content, the amount of glutarate being extracted from the aqueous phases increased until reaching a plateau at 58.1% glutarate yield (for molar ratios of T-C6/glutarate=9/1 or higher).
The use of both WSA's as amine extractant led to maximum re-extraction yields of 99%.
flux enforcement coupling growth to L-glutamic acid production by C. glutamicum provided the basis to select strains growing and producing faster by adaptive laboratory evolution.

One reason that a full recovery of glutarate from fermentation broth could not be achieved in the initial reactive extraction step is the presence of strong electrolytes like chloride ions in the aqueous phase
choosing sulfuric acid in this study to adjust the pH of the aqueous phase before reactive extraction was beneficial for the efficiency of the process.
ALE of C. glutamicum strain GluA T0 was carried out in repeated batch cultivations.
Whole-genome sequencing was performed with isolated gDNA from the originating strain C. glutamicum GluA T0 and the evolved strains GluA T5 and GluA T7.
SNP detection in all coding sequences of C. glutamicum was performed in readXplorer using 90% minimum percentage of variation and a minimum of 20 Reads as thresholds.
The apparent activities of GABA transaminase GabT and succinate semialdehyde oxidoreductase GabD were assayed in combination by monitoring NADPH formation after the addition of 5AVA.
To compare the different GabD variants, glutarate was added in different concentrations of 0 mM, 10 mM, 20 mM, 30 mM and 40 mM, respectively.
To determine the $K_{m}$ glutamine was added in different concentrations of 0 mM, 0.1 mM, 0.25 mM, 0.5 mM, 1 mM, 2 mM, 3.5 mM, 5 mM and 10 mM, respectively.
A baffled bioreactor with total a volume of 3.7 L was used (KLF, Bioengineering AG, Switzerland).
SNP detection in all coding sequences of C. glutamicum was performed in readXplorer using 90% minimum percentage of variation and a minimum of 20 Reads as thresholds.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Improve glutarate production by Corynebacterium glutamicum using adaptive laboratory evolution with flux enforcement.

canonical_events.summary: Low volumetric productivity of metabolically engineered C. glutamicum strain GluA producing glutarate via synthetic pathway.
Flux enforcement by gdh deletion couples growth to glutarate production, enabling selection of faster-growing mutants with improved production via ALE.
Serendipitous discovery of faster-growing variant GluA T0 with GabD^P134L mutation, prompting systematic ALE experiment.
ALE serial transfer experiment from GluA T0 to T8, selecting for faster growth under flux enforcement.
Growth rate increased from 0.10 to 0.17 h⁻¹ by T5, plateauing thereafter; glutarate titer increased 10% and 5AVA tenfold by T7; volumetric productivity doubled to 0.18 g L⁻¹ h⁻¹.
Transcriptome analysis of GluA T5 and T7 not instructive for explaining improved production.
Genome sequencing of GluA T5 revealed no mutations but increased plasmid copy numbers; GluA T7 had two mutations: 21-bp deletion in tetA(Z) and SNP gltB^E686Q.

Reverse genetics: introducing gltB^E686Q alone (GluA RG2) improved growth and productivity to levels comparable to GluA T7; mutated plasmid alone (GluA RG1) reduced performance; combining both (GluA RG3) did not further improve.
Beneficial effect of GltB^E686Q depends on gdh deletion; mutation restored lysine production in gdh deletion background but had no effect without gdh deletion.
Batch bioreactor comparison: GluA T7 outperformed GluA T0 in growth rate (0.19 vs 0.11 h⁻¹), titer (4.3 vs 2.0 g/L), productivity (0.21 vs 0.05 g/L/h), and yield (0.10 vs 0.04 g/g).
Fed-batch bioreactor with GluA T7 achieved 22.7 g/L glutarate, yield 0.23 g/g, productivity 0.35 g/L/h.
Reactive extraction optimization: T-C6 with 10 wt% 1-dodecanol in ethyl oleate achieved 58.1% glutarate yield; re-extraction with n-propylamine or n-butylamine achieved up to 99% yield.
Mechanism of GltB^E686Q not fully understood; further improvements possible by combining with other strategies.

edges.reason: Problem of low productivity motivates hypothesis of flux enforcement.
Hypothesis leads to design of ALE experiment, serendipitous discovery of GabD^P134L.
Discovery of GluA T0 prompts systematic ALE serial transfer experiment.
ALE experiment yields growth and productivity improvements.
Transcriptome analysis attempted to explain improvements but failed.
Genome sequencing performed to identify causal mutations.
Mutations identified lead to reverse genetics validation.
Reverse genetics confirms gltB^E686Q is causal and its dependence on gdh deletion.
Validated mutation enables bioreactor scale-up testing.
Batch results lead to fed-batch for higher titer and productivity.
Limitations noted after overall process evaluation.

must_cover_points: Flux enforcement by gdh deletion couples growth to glutarate production.
Serendipitous GabD^P134L mutation improved growth and production.
ALE serial transfer experiment from GluA T0 to T8.
Growth rate and productivity improvements plateaued at T7.
Genome sequencing revealed gltB^E686Q as causal mutation.
Reverse genetics confirmed gltB^E686Q benefit depends on gdh deletion.
Evolved strain GluA T7 outperformed parent in batch and fed-batch bioreactors.
Downstream processing optimization achieved high extraction yields.
